s1_b1: You just found g equals big G times Earth's mass over its radius squared. Now do something audacious. Flip it around.
s1_b2: How could anyone weigh the entire Earth? You can't lift it. You can't put it on a scale. It seems impossible.
s1_b3: But you don't have to lift it. The equation already holds the Earth's mass inside it — just solve for that instead.
s1_b4: Multiply across, and the Earth's mass stands alone: it equals g, times the radius squared, divided by big G.
s1_b5: And every piece on the right, you already know. g is nine-point-eight — you measured it by dropping a stone. r-Earth — measured by the ancient Greeks. And big G — Cavendish, with two weights on a thread.
s1_b6: Put them in. Out comes six, times ten to the twenty-fourth, kilograms. You just weighed the planet — without ever lifting it.
s1_b7: And it doesn't stop at Earth. The same trick weighs the sun, the planets, anything you can orbit or stand near. Cavendish measured a thread in a room — and held a planet on a number.
s1_b8: No codes. m-Earth, the planet's mass — the thing we wanted. g and r, what we could measure standing on it. Big G, the thread in Cavendish's room. Read it: the equation that weighs worlds.
